'''Lee Paul Briers''' (born 14 June 1978) is a professional [[rugby league]] coach who is a development coach at [[St Helens R.F.C.|St Helens]] in the [[Super League]] and assistant coach of the [[England national rugby league team|England national team]]. <ref>{{Cite web |date=2024-09-06 |title=Lee Briers finally confirmed as Saints' new assistant coach |url=https://www.sthelensstar.co.uk/sport/24568364.lee-briers-confirmed-st-helens-new-assistant-coach/ |access-date=2024-09-07 |website=St Helens Star |language=en}}</ref>

A former [[Great Britain national rugby league team|Great Britain]] and [[Wales national rugby league team|Wales]] international, he played the majority of his career at [[Warrington Wolves]] in the [[Super League]] as a {{rlp|so}} or {{rlp|sh}}. He won three [[Challenge Cup]] finals during his time at the club.<ref name="Profile at loverugbyleague.com"/><ref name="Statistics at rugbyleagueproject.org"/>

==Club career==

Widnes vikings (1996)
Played with Stuart Johnson
===St Helens (1997)===
====Loan to Carcassonne (1996–97)====
Briers' first experience of first team rugby was on loan at [[AS Carcassonne]] for the 1996–97 [[French Rugby League Championship]] season. While at home for Christmas leave, he played a friendly for St Helens in their [[Boxing Day]] Challenge Match against [[Wigan Warriors]].<ref name="Carcassonne">{{cite web|website=Love Rugby League|date=12 Mar 2021|accessdate=29 Jun 2024|title=Lee Briers: My time in Carcassonne was the making of me|url=https://www.loverugbyleague.com/post/lee-briers-my-time-in-carcassonne-was-the-making-of-me}}</ref>

====Return to parent club====
Briers made his competitive [[St Helens R.F.C.|St. Helens]] on 8 February 1997 in the fourth round match of the [[1997 Challenge Cup]] game against Wigan at the age of 18, standing in for suspended captain [[Bobbie Goulding]] after an emergency player recal from his loan at Carcassonne.<ref>{{cite web | url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints/player.php?num=14965 | title=Saints Heritage Society }}</ref> Saints won the game 28–12.

From his debut to April of the 1997 season, Briers made six appearances for St Helens, scoring one try and 24 goals. He was dropped from the starting line-up following the return of Goulding.<ref>{{cite web|last1=de la Rivière|first1=Richard|title=Lee Briers|url=http://richarddelariviere.co.uk/?p=403|publisher=WordPress|access-date=15 July 2017|date=28 December 2010}}</ref>

===Warrington (1997–2013)===
[[File:Lee Briers Warrington.jpg|thumb|Beiers taking a conversion for Warrington in 2012]]

Briers signed for [[Warrington Wolves]] in April 1997 for a fee of £65,000,<ref>{{cite web|last1=Hadfield|first1=Dave|title=Briers signs for Warrington|url=https://www.independent.co.uk/sport/briers-signs-for-warrington-1268078.html|website=The Independent|access-date=15 July 2017}}</ref> declining a three-year deal from [[AS Carcassonne]] in the process.<ref name="Carcassonne" /> He was named Young Player of the Year in his first season at [[Wilderspool Stadium]].

Briers was named as captain in 2003 and became known for his excellent kicking skills and his ability to successfully convert drop goals. He currently holds the Super League record, and jointly Warrington all-time record (with Paul Bishop), for the most drop goals in a game (5 against [[Halifax R.L.F.C.|Halifax]] at [[the Shay]] in 2002).<ref>{{cite web |last1=Hadfield |first1=Dave |title=Cardiss has the drop on Warrington |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/sport/general/rugby-league/cardiss-has-the-drop-on-warrington-190035.html |website=The Independent |access-date=2 March 2025 |date=26 May 2002}}</ref>

Briers' [[testimonial match]] at [[Warrington Wolves|Warrington]] took place in 2007. He stepped down as captain at the end of the 2007 season.<ref>{{cite web |title=Morley gets Warrington captaincy |url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/7193535.stm |website=BBC Sport |access-date=2 March 2025 |date=17 January 2008}}</ref>

Briers played in the [[2010 Challenge Cup Final]] victory over the [[Leeds Rhinos]] at [[Wembley Stadium]].<ref name="Leeds 6-30 Warrington">{{cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/rugby_league/8938067.stm|title=Leeds 6–30 Warrington|publisher=BBC|date=28 August 2010|access-date=22 November 2019|first=Ged|last=Scott}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2010/aug/28/leeds-rhinos-warrington-wolves-challenge-cup-final |location=London |work=The Guardian |title=Chris Hicks hat-trick leads Warrington to Challenge Cup triumph over Leeds |first=Andy |last=Wilson |date=28 August 2010 |access-date=22 November 2019<!--DASHBot--> |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100831105934/http://www.guardian.co.uk/sport/2010/aug/28/leeds-rhinos-warrington-wolves-challenge-cup-final |archive-date=31 August 2010 |url-status=live |df=dmy }}</ref>

The 2011 Super League season was Briers' 14th. During this season he broke a number of club records including top all-time points scorer, having overtaken club greats [[Brian Bevan]], and [[Steve Hesford]]. He broke the record during a Challenge Cup home 112–0 demolition of [[Swinton Lions|Swinton]].<ref name="Warrington 112-0 Swinton">{{cite web|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2011/may/21/lee-briers-warrington-swinton|title=Warrington 112–0 Swinton|publisher=Guardian|date=21 May 2011|access-date=22 July 2011}}</ref> This match also saw Briers break his own club record for points in a match (set 11 years earlier against York), with 44 points, from 16 goals and three tries.

He played in the [[2012 Challenge Cup Final]] victory over the [[Leeds Rhinos]] at [[Wembley Stadium]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2012/aug/25/leeds-warrington-challenge-cup |title=Warrington's battered Brett Hodgson recovers to see off Leeds in final|date=25 August 2012|work=Guardian UK|access-date=20 November 2019}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/19362692|title=Leeds Rhinos 18–35 Warrington Wolves|date=25 August 2012|work=BBC Sport|access-date=20 November 2019}}</ref>

He played in the [[2012 Super League Grand Final]] defeat by the [[Leeds Rhinos]] at [[Old Trafford]].<ref>{{cite news|title=Grand Final: Warrington 18-26 Leeds|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/19805055|access-date=20 November 2019|newspaper=BBC Sport|date=6 October 2012}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2012/oct/06/warrington-leeds-grand-final|title=Leeds' Kevin Sinfield stars in Grand Final triumph against Warrington|publisher=Guardian|date=6 October 2012|access-date=20 November 2019}}</ref>

In 2013, during the second match of the season against Wigan Warriors, Lee suffered a neck injury which saw him sidelined for 13 games. He returned for Warrington's Challenge Cup fifth round tie against Salford City Reds in which he made scored one try and kicked his 1,000th career goal.{{cn|date=June 2024}}

He played in the [[2013 Super League Grand Final]] defeat by the [[Wigan Warriors]] at [[Old Trafford]].<ref>{{cite news|last=Newsum|first=Matt|title=Super League Grand Final: Wigan Warriors beat Warrington|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/24384930|access-date=13 November 2019|newspaper=BBC Sport|date=5 October 2013}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url = https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2013/oct/05/warrington-wigan-super-league-grand-final|title= Wigan see off Warrington in X-rated Grand Final to complete double|date=5 October 2013|work=Guardian |access-date=13 November 2019}}</ref>

In November 2013, although Briers had a year remaining on his contract, he announced his retirement due to a neck injury. He played 425 games for Warrington, scoring a club record 2,586 points.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/24874806|title=Lee Briers: Warrington Wolves half-back announces retirement|work=BBC Sport|date=8 November 2013|access-date=1 January 2014}}</ref> Shortly before announcing his retirement, Briers released his autobiography, ''Off the Cuff''.

In 2018, Briers was inducted into Warrington's Hall of Fame.<ref>{{cite web |title=Lee Briers and Steve Hesford enter Warrington Wolves Hall of Fame |url=https://www.warringtonguardian.co.uk/sport/16192661.lee-briers-steve-hesford-enter-warrington-wolves-hall-fame/ |website=Warrington Guardian |access-date=2 March 2025 |date=29 April 2018}}</ref>

==International career==
Having made his [[Wales national rugby league team|Wales]] début in 1998, Briers went on to make 23 appearances for his country and featured in the [[2000 Rugby League World Cup]]. Briers was capped by Great Britain against France in their 42–12 win on 26 October 2001. 

Following the Wales team's failure to qualify for the [[2008 Rugby League World Cup]], Briers announced his international retirement.<ref>{{cite web |title=Briers ends international career |url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/7469439.stm |website=BBC Sport |access-date=2 March 2025 |date=23 June 2008}}</ref>

Briers came out of international retirement to represent Wales in the [[2010 European Cup]], and captained the side to victory in the tournament. He went on to captain Wales in the [[2011 Four Nations]], before once again retiring from international rugby league at the tournament's end.<ref name="Briers announces international retirement">{{cite web|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/15693300|title=Briers announces international retirement|publisher=bbc.co.uk|date=31 December 2017|access-date=1 January 2018}}</ref>

==Coaching career==
Following his retirement as a player, Briers became a youth coach at [[Warrington Wolves]] before being promoted to Assistant First Team Head Coach in 2018.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/56833803 | title=Lee Briers: Warrington Wolves assistant coach to leave at end of season | date=21 April 2021 }}</ref> In 2021 he was signed by [[Wigan Warriors]] as an Assistant Coach following the sacking of [[Adrian Lam]]. Briers stayed for one season in which Wigan won the [[2022 Challenge Cup]].<ref>{{cite web | url=https://wiganwarriors.com/blog/2022/08/09/lee-briers-to-leave-wigan/ | title=Lee Briers to leave Wigan | date=9 August 2022 }}</ref> He was signed by [[Brisbane Broncos]] as a Development Coach ahead of the 2023 season.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.broncos.com.au/news/2022/08/09/briers-appointed-development-coach-for-2023/ | title=Briers Appointed Development Coach for 2023 | date=9 August 2022 }}</ref> Following the [[2023 NRL Grand Final]], in which Brisbane finished runners-up, Briers was appointed Assistant Coach of the [[England national rugby league team|England national team]] ahead of the [[2023 Tonga rugby league tour of England|2023 Tonga tour of England]] in addition to his role at Brisbane.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.rugby-league.com/article/62126/lee-briers-named-as-england-mens-assistant-coach | title=Lee Briers named as England Men's Assistant Coach }}</ref> Briers was not released by Brisbane for England's 2024 mid-season friendly against [[France national rugby league team|France]].<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.loverugbyleague.com/post/nrl-player-availability-france-selection-policy-key-takeaways-from-shaun-wanes-england-press-call | title=Key takeaways from Shaun Wane England call including NRL availability | date=17 June 2024 }}</ref>
